data.title: ISM Services PMI: Strongest Growth In 6 Months
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Institute for Supply Management (ISM) released its August Services Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI), with the headline composite index at 52.0, the highest level in six months. This was higher than the forecast of 50.9 and keeps the index in expansion territory for a third straight month.

Here is an excerpt from the report summary:

Miller continues, “August’s Services PMI® level showed greater strength, driven by faster expansion rates for the Business Activity and New Orders indexes. Offsetting these positive indicators, however, are continued contraction in the Employment Index, a 16-year low for the Backlog of Orders Index, and the Prices Index remaining near 70 percent. Commentary once again was led by respondents’ increasing citations of tariff impacts, with some indication that business activity and imports are being driven by an attempt to get ahead of additional price increases while preparing for the holiday peak season.”

Here is a table showing the PMI's components.

Unlike its much older kin, the ISM manufacturing series, there is relatively little history for ISM's non-manufacturing data, especially for the headline composite Index, which dates from 2008. The chart below shows the non-manufacturing composite.

The more interesting and useful sub-component is the non-manufacturing business activity Index. The latest data point for August is 55.0, the highest level in five months.

For a diffusion index, this can be an extremely volatile indicator, hence the addition of a six-month moving average to help us visualize the short-term trends.

Theoretically, this indicator should become more useful as the time frame of its coverage expands. Manufacturing may be a more sensitive barometer than non-manufacturing activity, but we are increasingly a services-oriented economy, which explains our intention to keep this series on the radar.

ISM PMI Comparison: Manufacturing vs. Services

The next chart offers a comparison between the two Purchasing Managers' Indexes provided by the Institute for Supply Management. See the latest update on the Manufacturing PMI here.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.description:

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

data.title: Oscar Health: The Industry Is Having A Cold But 2026 Looks To Be Much Better
data.description:
Executive Summary

Oscar Health Inc. (OSCR) has grown significantly since its IPO in 2021 and was profitable in 2024. However, the company ran into some issues in 2025 but should be on the profitable path again in 2026. The technology platform is not only helping the company thrive but is also being sold to similar firms through +Oscar. The company is still heavily owned and run by its founders, Mario Schlosser and Joshua Kushner. The stocks for all the health insurance companies have taken a severe beating in 2025, and valuations are historically low. Oscar has built a decent cushion to survive through 2025 and is now a large company with $12 billion in revenue but only $4.3 billion in market capitalization. The company will increase its premium rate significantly for 2026, which is not just happening for OSCR, but an industry-wide phenomenon. The risk-reward seems very tilted in favor of substantial reward as the company turns profitable once again in 2026, and therefore, I’m initiating a Strong Buy.

Background

Oscar Health was founded in 2012 (was known as Mulberry Health Inc. and switched to Oscar Health in 2021) by Mario Schlosser, who was the CEO before hiring Mark Bertolini, and who is still the Chief Technology Officer; Kevin Nazemi, who has no role anymore; and Joshua Kushner, who was the largest financial backer by then and is still the largest owner. The guys were classmates at Harvard Business School and graduated in 2007. Oscar Health went public in March 2021, and the stock started trading on the NYSE under ticker OSCR at a price of $39/share. The valuation was $7.9 billion. The stock fell 10% on the first day and was down 94% at one point in 2023. The market cap is now around $4.3 billion at $17/share with 258.5 million A & B shares.

The company offers health insurance plans, predominantly through the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act, ACA, serving individuals, families, and employees. The company is technology-driven and not only offers support to its own insurance business but also provides technology to other providers and payors through +Oscar. Their platform, both online and mobile application, makes the onboarding and claims process more efficient, and according to its latest 10K, the company claims its business is “built upon our focus on member experience and our cloud-native technology platform.” The company had, as of June 20, 2025, over 2 million members and 2.9 billion in revenue for the last quarter. Oscar Health has its headquarters in Manhattan, NY.

Ownership and Governance

The company has 223 million Common A shares outstanding and 35.5 million Common B shares. The As are trading on the NYSE under ticker OSCR, but the Bs are not publicly traded. Class B is primarily held by the founders, notably Kushner through his firm Thrive Capital. The voting power is 20 to 1, but the same economics. After the IPO in 2021, Kushner owned 32.9 million shares of the B shares, and Schlosser owned 6.9 million B shares. At the end of Dec 2024, the founders owned, according to the 10K, 19.7% of the company and 79.9% of the voting power. Kushner is the majority owner and owns 17% of the company and 71.8% of the voting power; he holds 6.3 million A shares and most of the Common B. The largest owner of the A shares is Vanguard Group with 19.7 million A shares, or 7.6% of the company, followed by BlackRock with 16.6 million shares, or 6.4% of the company. The third largest is JP Morgan, with 13.8 million shares, or 5.3%. The next two largest hold both 11.8 million shares, or 4.6% of the company, and they are Deerfield Management Company and T. Rowe Price. The short interest is high with 62.5 million shares, or 24.2% of total shares outstanding, counting the B shares, or 28% excluding the B shares.

The company hired Mark Bertolini as the CEO in April 2023. He was the co-CEO of Bridgewater Associates, one of the largest hedge funds in the world. Before Bridgewater, he was the CEO of Aetna from 2010 until Aetna was sold to CVS in Nov 2018. Mario Schlosser had worked at Bridgewater and was able to get to know Mark before hiring him to take over his role as the CEO (from the 20VC podcast). The CFO is Scott Blackley since Aug 2023, and he was previously the CFO at Capital One (COF). And as mentioned, the co-founder Mario Schlosser has been the CTO since 2023, and he was the CEO before.

The board of directors consists of nine people. The chairman of the board is Jeff Boyd, former CEO at Priceline. He was a director of the board since 2015 and became chairman in 2021. The vice chairman is Joshua Kushner, co-founder since the start of the company in 2012. Mario Schlosser and Mark Bertolini are also on the board.

Business Model

The company is in the business of writing insurance premiums. They primarily offer individual health plans through health insurance marketplaces, established by the Affordable Care Act (ACA) and operated by the federal government, as well as by states. Individuals can also purchase policies off-exchange. Oscar is offering five different plans under categories defined by the ACA, which are, Catastrophic, Bronze, Silver, Gold, and Platinum. The company is focused on a fully digitized, consumer-centric model and was investing early in technology and member engagement, and it has paid off with large member growth.

In general, an insurer needs to do four things. They need to acquire premium revenue, identify and try to reduce risk per member, authorize care, and manage the cost of care delivery. If you can master all four, you will have a successful business.

Oscar looks to have mastered acquiring premium as they have grown significantly in the last few years, through a modernized, user-friendly, and more customized approach. For instance, the company was first to offer $0 access to 24/7 telehealth for urgent care needs going back all the way to 2014. The company has also connected with its members, as 42% of its members are monthly users, through its digital app. The platform can also handle clinical and non-clinical conversations, and through its CRM tool, Rosco, it can guide members to meet their needs. The company’s tools are also good at figuring out the regulatory framework, and this specific technology should better identify and reduce risk per member.

The company’s development of specific insurance software has led them to sell the services to other insurance companies. However, this business is still only a small part of revenue.

Financial Performance

The company has grown substantially since the IPO in 2021 at a $7.9 billion valuation. Revenue was $1.9 billion in 2021 and is currently $10.5 billion LTM June 2025, and the company is guiding $12 billion for 2025. The last five-year CAGR, compounded annual growth rate, is 80.8%, and the last three-year CAGR is 53.2%. This growth has been achieved by growing members with ACA insurance plans. They had 578k members at the end of 2021, and at the end of 2Q25, they had over 2 million members. However, revenue and member growth have been expensive, as the company only became profitable in 2024. However, last quarter was a loss again, and the company is guiding for a loss for all of 2025 as well.

The company didn’t price its insurances appropriately for the medical loss rate experienced starting in the second quarter of 2025. However, the company believes resubmitting rate filings for 2026 in states covering approximately 98% of current membership to reflect the higher market risk scores in the ACA marketplace should lead the company to be profitable again in 2026. The medical loss rate went from 79% in 2Q24 to 91.1% in 2Q25 and was only 75.4% in 1Q25. This was the major reason for going from $275 million in net income in 1Q25 and $56 million in 2Q24 to a loss of $228 million in 2Q25.

On the positive side, SG&A as a percentage of revenue has come down as the company has been able to leverage its costs as they have grown. Last quarter the rate stood at 18.7% vs. 19.6% in 2Q24, and the company is guiding for it to reach 17.1% to 17.6% for 2025. As the company's price, its insurance policies, better for 2026, continues to attract more members due to its user-friendly process and leveraging its SG&A cost, losses should flip to profits again. There are some reports already that the average price increase for 2026 is close to 20%. The company should be able to achieve a 10% SG&A rate and 85% MLR, which means a 5% profit rate. That equals $600 million in net income on $12 billion in revenues.

Oscar Health has some revenues outside premiums, mostly from the +Oscar platform, but in relation it’s a smaller business. Last quarter the company made $6.5 million vs. the quarter year over year of $5.2 million. However, that’s not even 1% of total revenue.

The company was free cash flow positive in 2024 and built a solid liquidity cushion to sustain losses over the next couple of quarters. The parent company seems to be in good shape too when you read through the financials in the 10K and latest 10Q. As of June 2025, the parent company held $205.1 million in cash and investments, up from $189.8 million at the end of December 2024. The cash and investment amount at the various subsidiaries was $5208.9 million at the end of June 2025 and $3808 million at the end of December, and the combined statutory capital at the insurance level was estimated to be $1.2 billion and estimated to have $579 million of excess capital above the minimum requirement.

The company has a $305 million Convertible Senior Note due in 2031 paying a 7.25% rate, payable in semi-annual cash. It was a private placement issued in February 2022, and one of the holders is Thrive Capital (Joshua Kushner). The notes are convertible into 120.1721 shares per $1000 notes, equivalent to an initial conversion price of $8.32 per share of Class A common stock. The company also has a $115 million revolving credit facility with Wells Fargo due at the end of 2025 but has nothing drawn as of the end of June 2025.

Industry Issues

The health insurance industry in the US was $1.7 trillion in 2023, and according to Grand View Research will grow at 6% CAGR and be $2.5 trillion in 2030. The largest part is public, with the government providing insurance such as Medicare and Medicaid. Medicare, which is the largest, is a federal government scheme that offers health insurance for people aged 65 years or older, with around 55 million covered. The largest companies in the industry by written premiums are listed below, and the largest is UnitedHealth Group (UNH) and stock is down ~40% year to date. Other public companies are, Elevance Health (ELV), Centene Corp. (CNC), Humana (HUM), CVS Health (CVS), Cigna Group (CI), Molina Healthcare, (MOH). Most of those stocks are down double digits year to date. The list is from 2023, when OSCR “only” had $5.7 billion in premiums vs. now $10.5 billion and is guiding for $12 billion in 2025, which would have given them spot 17.

The Affordable Care Act (ACA), or Obamacare, is a comprehensive healthcare reform law that aims to make health insurance more affordable and accessible to more Americans. As of Feb 2024, there were 20.8 million enrolled with active policies and paid premiums. The uninsured has fallen drastically due to this program; about 14-16% of people in the years leading up to the law enacted in March 2010, were uninsured. The percentage by 2023 was a record low at 7.7%. The funding comes mainly from the government, as most people buying Obamacare health insurance get help paying for it in the form of subsidies from the federal government. People purchase these private health insurance plans through each state’s health insurance exchange. People who enroll in coverage through the exchange will either be directed to Medicaid or a private plan, depending on their income. Although Medicaid is a government-run program, it's typically administered by private insurance companies. So through this process, lower-income households might just get Medicaid-funded insurance. The enrollment process is only open from Nov 1st to Jan 15th and will take effect either Jan 1st, Feb 1st, or March 1st. If you end up getting Medicaid, it will start immediately. There is also a limited special enrollment period depending on your circumstances, like a change of marital status or birth of a child, then the coverage will take effect the month after you enroll.

In the new “One Big Beautiful Bill” signed into law in July 2025, there are some changes to who will be eligible for Medicaid and who will have access to ACA plans. There is a new “community engagement requirement,” which means people who want to continue to have coverage under Medicaid will have to verify 80 hours per month of working, volunteering, or attending school. There will be new requirements as well for people covered by the ACA plans, which are mostly in the renewal process, which won’t be automatic, and you will have to update info every year. The biggest change is the new law doesn’t extend the ACA premium tax credits, which are set to expire at the end of 2025, and would most likely increase the premium. This will certainly affect Oscar Health, and it is something to be concerned over. There is still a chance these tax credits might be extended. The state officials will most likely urge Congress to extend them.

Another issue for the industry is that healthcare costs in general have been on the rise, such as hospital expenses and prescription drugs.

Value Proposition

The value proposition for Oscar Health (OSCR) is the size of the premiums and the low market capitalization. The company is guiding for $12 billion in revenues for 2025, and with the rate increase for next year (2026), earnings should be at least 5% of revenues, or $600 million. The valuation seems too low with a $4.3 billion market capitalization.

The company is carrying a lot of cash, but most of it is for statutory purposes, and claimed excess cash was $579 million. The parent cash is $205 million, so if we use debt of $300 million (the convertible notes) minus parent cash of $205 million, the company's net debt is $95 million. The revenue growth in the last two quarters was 42% for 1Q25 and 29% for 2Q25. If we assume 20% revenue growth and turning profitable in 2026, one would be conservative in assuming a slightly less than market multiple of 1x sales and 20x EPS, which leads to ~$46/share at $12 billion in revenue and ~$53/share at $14 billion in revenue (20% topline growth in 2026).

Catalysts

The biggest catalyst coming is the changes in the Affordable Care Act, and they are mostly negative. However, the stock is probably pricing in the worst-case scenario, and it’s highly likely we will see an extension of tax credits to phase in. The other big catalyst, which is positive, is the rate increase for premiums charged in 2026, which should make the company profitable again.

Risks

The largest risk for the company is the concentration of ACA plan members. As mentioned, there are new rules that might make it harder to access ACA insurance. Another risk is continued losses in 2026/27 and negative free cash flow.

data.title: FDD: Caution Warranted Heading Into A Tough Seasonal Stretch
data.description:
The EURUSD currency pair hovers near its best level going back to 2021. That’s a boon for US investors owning ex-US shares and foreign ETFs denominated in dollars.

As such, we’ve seen tremendous alpha among many international market funds; specifically, European Financials stand out. I was also intrigued by a recent report from Goldman Sachs that pointed out strength in European value stocks.

One ETF, now ranked No. 1 in its Sub Class, per Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, has seen a strong volume influx over the last handful of months.

The First Trust STOXX® European Select Dividend Index Fund (NYSEARCA:FDD) sports robust momentum and a low price-to-earnings ratio, but its earnings growth is questionable. Moreover, I’ll call out later in the article that the technicals are not all a green light.

EURUSD Holding Near Multi-Year Highs

GS: Value has outperformed in Europe, while growth has outperformed in the US

According to the issuer, FDD seeks investment results corresponding generally to the price and yield, before fees and expenses, of the STOXX Europe Select Dividend 30 Index. The Index is dividend-weighted, comprising 30 high-dividend-yielding stocks selected based on a positive five-year dividend-per-share growth rate, a dividend-to-earnings-per-share ratio of 60% or less, and an "outperformance factor" calculated from net dividend yield, with each component’s weight capped at 15%.

FDD is a small ETF with just $673 million in assets under management as of September 3, 2025. Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 59 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is high at 4.7%.

Share-price momentum has been stellar throughout 2025, earning the product a pristine A ETF Grade from Seeking Alpha’s quant system. The fund returned more than 50% from its early-year low to the 2025 peak. Today, however, some technical signals point to at least a consolidation. More on that to come.

In the here and now, FDD sports a decent ETF Risk Grade, thanks to its somewhat muted annualized volatility trend lately. I’ll caution prospective investors that its basket, heavy into European Financials, can be volatile when global growth worries perk up. Still, with average daily volume of more than 400,000 shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of six basis points, liquidity is fine.

Looking closer at the portfolio, the 4-star, Silver-rated ETF by Morningstar has very high exposure to both large-cap value and mid-cap value when scanning the style box. Just 14% of the allocation is classified as blend, and there is no growth access. That makes FDD is material factor wager on blue chips beating story stocks.

Value investors are likely intrigued by both the high yield and P/E ratio. But take a look at the long-term EPS growth rate of just 4.9%. The resulting PEG ratio is actually a bit pricey at more than 2.0x. So, I’m not excited about the growth-adjusted valuation.

FDD: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

As for the sector breakdown, as hinted at earlier, FDD is significantly allocated to banks and other stocks from the cyclical-value Financials sector. That group has soared this year, thanks to GDP growth trends that are holding up in Europe, as well as the steepening yield curve trend seen across the Euro Area.

In terms of concentration, FDD’s top 10 holdings account for a notable, but not extreme, 41% of total assets. Moreover, I expect single-stock volatility to be somewhat modest now through mid-October (that’s when Financials-sector earnings reports begin to roll in).

FDD: Holdings & Dividend Information

FDD has recently garnered fanfare, but the product has been around since before the Great Financial Crisis (GFC). When scanning price-only returns, we find that the September-October period has been shaky. So, being prudent with an entry now through the early part of the fourth quarter could be a good move.

FDD: Weak Sept-Oct Trends

The Technical Take

With a valuation that’s not as attractive as might seem at first blush, FDD’s technical situation augurs for some caution. Notice in the chart below that shares are close to multi-year highs, but that the RSI momentum oscillator has printed a series of lower highs. That’s bearish divergence in the eyes of a technical analyst. What’s more, the fund is now probing its rising short-term 50-day moving average, so that bears watching.

But take a look at the long-term 200dma—it's on the rise, signaling that the bulls control the primary trend. Thus, the chart is by no means bearish. Additionally, with a high amount of volume by price (as noted by the horizontal bars on the left side of the graph), there should be ample support if we see an extended pullback. Overall, support is down at $15—I think that mark could get tagged later this month.

FDD: Bearish RSI Divergence, Rising 200dma, $15 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on FDD. This European dividend fund is heavily invested in Financials, while the valuation is not all that compelling on a growth-adjusted basis. Technicals, meanwhile, point to a continued retreat off the multi-year high.

data.title: IMTM: International Equities With Momentum
data.description:
Thesis

When the dollar is weak and weakening, an investor needs to look at assets denominated in other currencies. Those assets will rise in value as the dollar depreciates, all else equal. The most liquid international investments are exchange listed equities and fixed income. In today's article we are going to discuss an equities exchange traded fund from BlackRock, namely the iShares MSCI Intl Momentum Factor ETF (NYSEARCA:IMTM).

The U.S. dollar outlook is negative

The U.S. dollar has lost more than -9% this year:

The ICE US Dollar Index (DXY) is down -9.4% in 2025, and the outlook is not rosy:

Sept 3 (Reuters) - The U.S. dollar will weaken over coming months as market participants ponder the Federal Reserve's future independence and how many more rate cuts it may deliver, a Reuters survey of foreign exchange strategists showed on Wednesday.

The greenback, down nearly 10% against a basket of major currencies this year, has been the worst performer among them. The short-dollar trade has dominated FX markets since late March, according to Commodity Futures Trading Commission data.

Fundamental factors such as interest rates and deficits are driving the move in the dollar, thus further weakness should not be a surprise.

Holding international equities

Let us start with the objective of the IMTM fund:

The iShares MSCI Intl Momentum Factor ETF seeks to track the investment results of an index that measures the performance of international developed large- and mid-capitalization stocks exhibiting relatively higher momentum characteristics.

The followed index is the MSCI World ex USA Momentum Index, and the ETF ends up with the following geographic composition:

  • Germany: 15.2%
  • Canada: 15%
  • Japan: 14.4%
  • UK: 12%
  • France: 7%
  • Spain: 6.6%
  • Australia: 5%
  • Italy: 5%
  • Switzerland: 4%
  • Singapore: 2.7%

Please note the index and ETF do not contain any U.S. equities, and the geographic composition is fairly granular, lacking significant individual country concentration.

The top names in the fund are large capitalization stocks from very well-known issuers:

Although SAP (SAP) and Rolls-Royce (RR) are the top holdings (a technology company and an industrials one), from a sectoral standpoint Financials represent 41% of the ETF. Financials are followed by Industrials at 20%, and Communication at 7%. A severe global financial crisis would be the largest risk factor here given the sectoral break-down.

In addition to selecting international equities only, the index reflects the performance of an equity momentum strategy. It identifies companies that have shown strong price performance over the past 6 to 12 months, on the premise that this outperformance is likely to continue in the near term. The momentum factor thesis is built on the academic principle that stocks with high momentum tend to keep rising. A momentum score is calculated for each stock by combining its 6-month and 12-month price performance, adjusted for volatility. Subsequently a fixed number of stocks with the highest positive momentum scores are selected. The index is rebalanced on a semi-annual basis.

In the next section we will compare the IMTM performance with a simple international equities ETF, as well as look at the ETF's analytics.

Performance

Now that we have seen what the ETF holds and how it is constructed, let us see if the end results are comparable with a simple equities ETF, and if the analytics are favorable:

We are comparing IMTM to the Vanguard Developed Markets Index Fund (VEA), a simple international equities ETF that is widely used by market participants. In the past 3 years we can see a very close correlation between the two names, with a slight outperformance by IMTM in the past year. The net total return shows IMTM with a +10% outperformance.

On a longer timeframe we get the following annualized returns from Morningstar:

  • IMTM: 5-year 10.2%, 10-year 9.1%
  • VEA: 5-year 10.5%, 10-year 8.2%

VEA comes with a 16% 3-year standard deviation and a 0.74 Sharpe, while IMTM comes with a 15% 3-year standard deviation and a 0.95 Sharpe. The fund has indeed delivered superior returns to VEA, with a lower volatility profile as well. The analytics are therefore very good.

One has to be careful with momentum factor funds, because sometimes certain names produce better results but with much higher risk and volatility via high beta equities. It is not the case here.

Let us also have a look at the historic drawdown profile for the two names:

We can notice from the above chart that in most instances IMTM (the blue line) has lower drawdowns when compared to VEA (the red line). We can see that occurring in October 2022, in March 2023, in October 2023 and in April 2025. The only outlier is August 2024 when the one-day market meltdown saw a larger move down in IMTM when compared to VEA.

Valuations are still appealing

Although IMTM does not have a static portfolio, and its components change as their momentum factor changes, we are still going to have a look at valuations. As investors we would like to see if the ETF makes its selections and ends up with high beta components, or the holdings are within acceptable valuation levels. Courtesy of Morningstar we get the following table:

The portfolio P/E is at 16x, a very acceptable level when compared to the S&P 500 at 22x and the international equities index at 15.5x. Other valuation metrics also show levels which are in-line with overall markets, such as P/B (price to book) at 2x and P/S at 1.8x.

As it stands the fund's valuation metrics do not indicate high beta equity selection, just overall names with normalized valuations that have an upward momentum.

Conclusion

With the dollar set for further weakness, international equities are a good place to invest. IMTM adds a momentum factor on top of a portfolio of international equities (U.S. stocks are not allowed in the portfolio) and has managed to outperform the standard in the space (i.e. VEA) in the past three years. The ETF has achieved its metrics with a lower volatility profile than VEA and narrower drawdowns. We like this BlackRock product and its analytics, and view it favorable in a weaker dollar environment.

data.title: S&P Global Services PMI: Growth Remains Positive In August
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The August U.S. Services Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) from S&P Global came in at 54.5, below the 55.4 forecast. The reading marks the 31st consecutive month of expansion and the second strongest growth of 2025 so far.

From the latest press release, Chris Williamson, Chief Business Economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence, said:

“Although weaker than signaled by the preliminary ‘flash’ PMI reading, and below that seen in July, the expansion of the service sector in August was still the second strongest recorded so far this year. Together with a robust manufacturing PMI reading, the surveys are consistent with the US economy growing at a solid 2.4% annualized rate in the third quarter.

“Fuller order books, reflecting a summer upturn in customer demand, has meanwhile encouraged service providers to take on additional staff in increasing numbers, accompanied by a return to hiring in the manufacturing sector. While low household confidence is reportedly keeping spending on consumer services relatively subdued, demand for financial services is showing especially strong growth amid improving financial market conditions.

“However, the brighter news on current economic growth and hiring is marred by concerns over future growth prospects and inflation. Business optimism regarding the year ahead outlook has dropped to one of the lowest levels seen over the past three years amid escalating worries over the uncertainty and drop in demand caused by federal government policy, most notably tariffs, as well as the associated rise in price pressures. Inflation concerns have been fanned by a further steep rise in input costs which have fed through to another marked increase in average charges for services.

“The survey data therefore point to some downside risks to growth in the coming months while signaling upside risks to inflation, as import tariffs feed through to prices charged for both goods and services.”

Background on the S&P Global US Services PMI

The S&P Global US Services PMI™ measures the activity level of purchasing managers in the services sector through a questionnaire of ~400 service sector companies. The sectors covered include consumer (excluding retail), transportation, information, communication, finance, insurance, real estate, and business services.

The S&P Services PMI is a diffusion index, meaning that a reading above 50 indicates expansion in the sector compared to the previous month and a reading below 50 indicates contraction.

Here is a snapshot of the series since mid-2012.

Here is an overlay with the equivalent PMI survey conducted by the Institute for Supply Management, which it refers to as "non-manufacturing" (see our full article on this series here). Over its history, the ISM metric has been significantly more volatile of the two.

The next chart uses a three-month moving average of the two rather volatile series to facilitate our understanding of the current trend.

S&P Global PMI Comparison: Manufacturing vs. Services

The next chart offers a comparison between the two Purchasing Managers' Indexes provided by S&P Global.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: First Commonwealth: Downgrading After Price Rally, Earnings Outlook Continues To Remain Satisfactory
data.description:
First Commonwealth Financial Corporation’s (NYSE: NYSE:FCF) stock price has rallied by around 13% since I upgraded the stock to a buy rating in my report in March 2025. The company’s earnings in 2025 will likely receive support from the recent acquisition, upcoming swap terminations, and Fed funds rate cuts. However, there are also some headwinds in the form of weak labor markets and housing markets, which will likely slow down the loan growth.

Labor Markets Point Towards Loan Growth Slowdown After a Good First Half

First Commonwealth’s loan portfolio grew by 6.5% in the first half of this year (12.9% annualized), a marked improvement from last year’s loan growth of just 0.16%. The acquisition of CenterBank in May 2025 added $196.9 million in average loans, as per details given in the earnings presentation. This contributed 2.2 percentage points to the total growth of 6.5 percentage points in the first half. The remainder of the growth came from organic means.

For the remainder of the year, I think loan growth will most probably slow down because of some weaknesses in First Commonwealth’s markets. The company mainly operates in the states of Pennsylvania and Ohio. Further, it has quite a diverse loan mix with commercial real estate and residential mortgages being the two biggest segments.

I’m not optimistic about the future growth of the commercial real estate segment. This is because weakness in the labor market indicates business activity is stalling. If businesses are reluctant to expand their teams, then they will be reluctant to expand facilities and make capex decisions as well. Nationwide job growth has been very low over the last four months since the announcement of the global tariffs, and it was even negative in June, according to the latest revised numbers shown in the chart below, taken from a news report.

Both Pennsylvania and Ohio saw a decline in total nonfarm jobs in August, as shown below.

Moreover, the Philly Fed Manufacturing Activity Index, which measures changes in business growth in the Pennsylvania, New Jersey, and Delaware region, was negative in August.

On the other hand, there is some good news for the residential mortgage segment as the house price trends in Pennsylvania and Ohio have flattened this year.

Nevertheless, the overall housing market is quite weak. In fact, according to the latest news reports quoting Redfin, the homeownership rate in the nation has fallen for the first time since 2016.

Overall, I’m expecting the loan portfolio to grow by 0.75% in each of the last two quarters of 2025, leading to full-year loan growth of 8.1%. I’m also expecting deposits to grow at nearly the same rate as loans.

Swap Terminations, Rate Cuts to Lift the Margin

First Commonwealth’s net interest margin surged in the second quarter by around 21 basis points. The growth was mostly attributable to a sharp increase in the average yield on loans, as mentioned in the earnings release.

The earning assets yield is likely to grow further because of some swap terminations ahead. According to details given in the earnings presentation, $250 million worth of receive fixed macro swaps will mature in 2025, and $175 million will mature in 2026. If the Fed cuts rates by 50 basis points in the remainder of this year, then the management estimates a cumulative benefit of approximately 7 basis points by the end of 2025.

The upcoming Fed funds rate cuts can also lift the margin. I’m expecting the Fed to cut rates by 25 basis points in September and then by another 25 basis points in December 2025. However, after the release of the low payroll numbers, CME’s FedWatch tool is showing that the market is mostly expecting up to 75 basis points of rate cuts.

I'm still not expecting 75 basis points of rate cuts because I think inflation will pick up pace soon because of the tariffs.

Rate cuts will likely benefit the margin in the near term because more deposits than loans will reprice shortly. Interest-bearing transaction and savings deposits made up 60% of total deposits at the end of June 2025. In contrast, variable-rate loans made up 51% of total loans, as mentioned in the presentation.

The management mentioned in the conference call that it expects the margin to rise from 3.83% in 2Q’25 to “the low to mid 3.90s by the end of the year, give or take a few basis points as always”. The management’s guidance was based on an outlook of 50 basis points of Fed funds rate cuts.

Keeping all these factors in mind, I’m expecting the net interest margin to grow by 10 basis points in the second half of 2025.

Expecting an EPS of $1.43 for 2025

The margin expansion and loan growth will likely be the key earnings drivers this year. The merger in 2Q’25 led to a spike in the non-interest expense and provision expense, which will drag the full year’s earnings. For the second half of the year, I’m expecting the provision expense to return to the average level seen in the four quarters of 2024 and the first quarter of 2025.

Further, I’m expecting the efficiency ratio to gradually improve from 58.2% in 2Q’25 to 56.8% in 4Q’25, which is close to last year’s average. I’m expecting an improvement from the second quarter’s level because merger-related expenses will not recur. Further, there are likely to be some cost savings from the merger.

As for non-interest income, I’m assuming it will remain stable at the second quarter’s level.

Overall, I’m expecting the company to report an EPS of $1.43 for 2025, up 2.5% year-over-year. In my last report, I gave an EPS projection of $1.41 for 2025. I haven’t changed my estimate much because the first half’s earnings were somewhat in line with expectations, and my outlook hasn’t changed too much.

Time for a Downgrade

I upgraded First Commonwealth Financial to a buy rating in March 2025. Since then, the stock price has grown by a good 13%. To update my investment thesis, I’m using the historical average price-to-earnings (“P/E”) and price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) multiples given in the chart below.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple by my EPS forecast gives a target price of $15.8 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple by my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $19.1 for the end of 2025.

The average price target for the end of 2025 comes out to $17.5, which is higher than my previous target of $16.8. My target price is higher now because the historical moving average multiples are higher.

My new target price suggests a 2.4% price downside from the September 5 closing price of $17.9. If I add the stock’s dividend yield of 3.0% to the price downside, then I get a total expected return of just 0.6%. Hence, I believe it’s time to downgrade First Commonwealth Financial to a hold rating.

The company’s earnings outlook is satisfactory as margin and loan growth will likely drive earnings. However, following the price rally since my last report, the stock is no longer attractively valued. Hence, it makes sense to downgrade First Commonwealth to a hold rating from my previous rating of buy.

data.title: Touchstone Balanced Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Recap

Uncertainty that rattled investors in the first quarter continued into the second quarter. A peak in tariff escalation occurred on April 2 (nicknamed Liberation Day) with the rollout of reciprocal tariffs across all trading partners. A brief reprieve followed, easing near-term market pressure but offering little clarity for businesses and consumers evaluating future expenditures and hiring plans.

Despite these headwinds, the economy showed resilience. Retail sales grew, though they exhibited some volatility due to preemptive buying ahead of tariff implementation. The labor market remained healthy, with job growth and real wage increases supporting consumers. Yet, a rising personal savings rate hinted at caution in response to ongoing policy risks. Housing remained a soft spot due to continued affordability challenges as both mortgage rates and home prices stayed elevated.

Markets were volatile but ended the quarter on a positive note. The S&P 500 briefly touched bear market territory before rebounding to record highs. Credit spreads followed a similar pattern initially widening, then tightening to levels below historical averages. This improving sentiment, coupled with eased inflation concerns, contributed to a steeper yield curve, while the 10-year Treasury yield remained relatively stable.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone Balanced Fund (Class A Shares, Load Waived) outperformed its blended benchmark, the 60% Russell 1000 Index and 40% Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, for the quarter ended June 30, 2025.

The Fund benefitted from its allocation decisions during the quarter, particularly its higher equity exposure, which outperformed fixed income. The Fund began the quarter modestly overweight in equities and increased that exposure further in April as markets temporarily declined.

On the equity side, selection detracted from results relative to the benchmark. In particular, holdings in Health Care and Information Technology (IT sector) weighed on performance. An overweight to UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) (Health Care sector) was a key detractor, as the stock underperformed. In contrast, underweight positions in Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) (IT sector) and Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) (IT sector) also detracted as those names advanced more strongly.

Fixed Income allocations contributed positively. The Fund took advantage of spread widening in April to add high yield corporates, which later performed well as spreads tightened. An overweight in Investment Grade corporates and strategic interest rate management also enhanced results, as the Fund navigated shifting growth and inflation expectations.

Positioning changes during the quarter included reallocating excess cash to equities in April and initiating a position in Las Vegas Sands Corp. (LVS) (Consumer Discretionary sector), while exiting positions in Alibaba (Consumer Discretionary sector) and FedEx (Industrials sector). In fixed income, beyond the high yield addition, duration positioning was tactically adjusted in response to rate volatility. The Fund maintained overweight positions in Investment Grade Credit and Securitized sectors, emphasizing quality and liquidity.

Outlook and Conclusion

The Fund continues to carry a slight equity overweight, balancing opportunity with risk as markets adjust to rich valuations and growing macroeconomic headwinds. While equity valuations have returned to elevated levels after a sharp rebound, the Fund is positioning with a moderately defensive stance while remaining ready to capture selective opportunities.

In equities, the Fund is overweight in Communication Services and Health Care, while underweight in Utilities, Industrials, and Consumer Discretionary. The focus remains on high-quality businesses with strong competitive advantages and sustainable returns on capital.

In fixed income, the Fund holds an underweight in U.S. Government securities and an overweight in Investment Grade Credit, with continued emphasis on high-quality issuers. The addition of high yield corporates in April reflects a tactical response to market dislocations, and the portfolio remains positioned with neutral duration relative to the benchmark.

Looking forward, expectations for economic growth in 2025 and 2026 have been lowered amid persistent uncertainty.

Still, the passage of a new reconciliation bill, referred to as One Big, Beautiful Bill, may help lift near-term activity through tax cuts and investment incentives.

The Fund is well-prepared to navigate a challenging environment. Should conditions stabilize, exposure to equities and credit sectors is expected to benefit. If volatility persists or growth slows further, the Fund is positioned to take advantage of market dislocations, supported by its high-conviction, bottom-up approach that seeks strong risk-adjusted opportunities across sectors.

Fort Washington is a member of Western & Southern Financial Group

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which is no guarantee of future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment in the Fund will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost.

The material may contain confidential information and unauthorized use, disclosure, copying, dissemination or redistribution is strictly prohibited. This is a Touchstone Investments presentation of the data, and FRC is not responsible for the formatting or configuration of this material or for any inaccuracy in the presentation thereof.

A Word About Risk

The Fund invests in equities which are subject to market volatility and loss. The Fund invests in stocks of large-cap companies which may be unable to respond quickly to new competitive challenges. The Fund invests in stocks of mid-cap companies which may be subject to more erratic market movements than stocks of larger, more established companies. The Fund invests in foreign securities, including depositary receipts, such as American Depositary Receipts, Global Depositary Receipts, and European Depositary Receipts, which carry the associated risks of economic and political instability, market liquidity, currency volatility and accounting standards that differ from those of U.S. markets and may offer less protection to investors. The Fund invests in emerging markets securities which are more likely to experience turmoil or rapid changes in market or economic conditions than developed countries. The Fund invests in preferred stocks which are relegated below bonds for payment should the issuer be liquidated. If interest rates rise, the fixed dividend on preferred stocks may be less attractive, causing their price to decline. The Fund invests in fixed-income securities which can experience reduced liquidity during certain market events, lose their value as interest rates rise and are subject to credit risk which is the risk of deterioration in the financial condition of an issuer and/or general economic conditions that can cause the issuer to not make timely payments of principal and interest also causing the securities to decline in value and an investor can lose principal. The Fund invests in investment grade debt securities which may be downgraded by a Nationally Recognized Statistical Rating Organization (NRSRO) to below investment grade status. The Fund invests in non-investment grade debt securities which are considered speculative with respect to the issuers’ ability to make timely payments of interest and principal, may lack liquidity and has had more frequent and larger price changes than other debt securities. The Fund invests in U.S. government agency securities which are neither issued nor guaranteed by the U.S. Treasury and are not guaranteed against price movements due to changing interest rates. The Fund invests in mortgage-backed securities and asset-backed securities which are subject to the risks of prepayment, defaults, changing interest rates and at times, the financial condition of the issuer. The Fund invests in derivatives and securities such as futures contracts, options, forward contracts and swap agreements. Derivatives can be highly volatile, illiquid and difficult to value, subject to counterparty and leverage risks and there is risk that changes in the value of a derivative held by the Fund will not correlate with the Fund’s other investments. Leverage can create an interest expense that may lower the Fund’s overall returns. There can be no guarantee that a leveraging strategy will be successful. Gains or losses from speculative positions in a derivative may be much greater than the original cost and potential losses may be substantial. The Fund invests in mortgage dollar rolls which involve increased risk and volatility, as the securities the Fund is required to repurchase may be worth less than the securities that the Fund originally held. The Fund may experience higher portfolio turnover which may lead to increased fund expenses, lower investment returns and higher short-term capital gains taxable to shareholders. Events in the U.S. and global financial markets, including actions taken to stimulate or stabilize economic growth may at times result in unusually high market volatility, which could negatively impact Fund performance and cause it to experience illiquidity, shareholder redemptions, or other potentially adverse effects. Banks and financial services companies could suffer losses if interest rates rise or economic conditions deteriorate. Current and future portfolio holdings are subject to change. The Adviser engages a sub-adviser to make investment decisions for the Fund’s portfolio; it may be unable to identify and retain a sub-adviser who achieves superior investment returns relative to other similar sub-advisers.

Original Post

data.title: Marriott International: Shares Now Better Value As Demand Concerns Weigh On Sentiment (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Like so many consumer-facing stocks, sentiment towards global lodging giant Marriott International (NASDAQ:MAR) appears to have shifted somewhat in recent quarters. Despite tracking for solid earnings per share growth, shares of the Maryland-based hotelier have fallen by around 6% since my last update in November, trailing the wider market by roughly 15 points.

Looking back, my only real issue with Marriott was its valuation, as the bout of post-election exuberance saw its shares gain over 10% in November alone. With that sending its price-to-earnings ratio over the 30x mark, prospective buyers were facing a real risk from a deflating valuation multiple - enough to offset the solid double-digit growth offered by its underlying business.

I do think these shares look better value today. The combination of a lower stock price and solid earnings growth has seen Marriott's P/E retreat back into the mid-20s area, offering investors a better chance of realizing reasonable investment returns. Evidence of softening demand in the U.S. is a risk to consider, but for the long-term investor, I would be inclined to see this as an opportunity.

Signs Of Softer Domestic Demand Emerge

While Marriott is a global business, the U.S. and Canada still collectively account for over 1 million, or ~62%, of its total rooms. And readers will not need reminding that these countries have seen a significant, tariff-driven shift in economic sentiment in the past couple of quarters.

That shift does appear to be creeping into Marriott's financial results. In the first half of the year, the company saw revenue per available room ('RevPAR') rise by 1.6% in the U.S. & Canada, a deceleration on the ~3% growth booked in H1 2024. RevPAR is a function of hotel occupancy levels and the average rate per room. The latter was still rising in H1, but occupancy levels declined, signaling weaker demand.

While economic uncertainty was already increasing in Q1, it obviously jumped considerably after April's "Liberation Day" tariff announcement. As a result, Q2 RevPAR growth was markedly weaker, coming in flat in the U.S. & Canada.

Positively, RevPAR outside of North America remains much more robust, growing by a healthy 5.3%, with occupancy and the average room rate both increasing. Still, given North America accounts for such a large share of its room count, the weaker performance there has caused management to downgrade guidance, with firm-wide RevPAR now seen rising 2% this year at the mid-point, down 100bps on initial 2025 guidance.

Earnings Are Still Growing

While the company is facing a slightly softer outlook than management envisioned at the start of the year, we should put this into some context. There is still plenty to be bullish about here. For one, RevPAR is still growing, albeit it is tracking below my estimate of its long-run average (around 3.5%, in line with nominal GDP growth).

Then there are the other drivers of Marriott's earnings to consider. Less than 1% of its rooms are in hotels that are owned or leased by the firm itself. It is really is fee-based business. This means that, in addition to RevPAR, earnings will largely be driven by the total number of rooms in its system. System growth remains robust, with net room growth clocking in at 4.7% year-on-year last quarter. Like other branded players, this implies that Marriott is continuing to gain market share, and this was confirmed by management on the Q2 earnings call.

A number of reasons explain why franchisees are attracted to branded players like Marriott, including their above-average occupancy rates, which is aided by their significant, growing loyalty programs (Marriott's has nearly 250 million members, up from around 210 million a year ago). Direct booking capabilities are another significant driver, as this results in cheaper customer acquisition costs. Put simply, while franchisees do have to sacrifice a cut of room revenue, it still makes financial sense for the, to partner with a strong brand like Marriott.

To that end, Marriott's room pipeline continues to grow. It stood at over 590,000 rooms at the end of last quarter, around 40% of which are already under construction. At around 33% of its existing system size, Marriott's current pipeline could sustain a ~4% annual growth rate for around 8 years, all else equal.

Cost leverage is another attractive feature of Marriott's business, as a growing fee stream does not require similar growth in operating expenses. This results in margin expansion over time. Indeed, at 65% last quarter, Marriott's adjusted operating income margin is now around 1,000bps higher than it was back in 2019. The upshot of this is that earnings tend to grow faster than revenue.

This was on display in Q2. While gross fee revenue rose 4% year-on-year to $1.4 billion, adjusted EBITDA growth was around 290 basis points higher, clocking in at nearly 7%. As a result, and even though guidance has been tightened, management still expects $5.35 billion in full-year EBITDA. That would be around 7.5% higher than it posted in 2024.

Finally, there is the capital-light nature of the fee business to consider. Being mainly a franchisor, Marriott doesn't have to invest much in order to grow its earnings. Hotel construction costs, for example, are paid by the property owners rather than Marriott. With that, full-year investment spending is seen at around $1.4 billion, equal to roughly 25% of its adjusted EBITDA, and note that this figure includes a $355 million acquisition.

Fundamentally, this allows Marriott to spend heavily on capital returns without compromising its growth. Indeed, management is guiding for roughly $4 billion in capital returns this year. That equates to a yield of around 5.5% based on the company's current market value.

The Valuation

Marriott trades for $267 per share as I type, around 7% lower on my last piece. Back then, Marriott was trading for around 31x then-2024 consensus EPS. I said I would be willing to pay around 25x for the stock. This has basically occurred, with the combination of higher earnings and a lower stock price leaving the shares at ~26.5x consensus 2025 EPS.

To be clear, this is still on the high side compared to its pre-pandemic range. COVID hit Marriott's earnings pretty hard, so I like to look at 2018-2019 figures for a cleaner insight. The company made $5.56 (2018) and $5.92 (2019) in adjusted EPS in those years, and as you can see, the stock price averaged around $130. This implies Marriott was trading on a P/E of roughly 22.5x.

Although its current multiple is higher than this, the key point is that its valuation can now absorb a modest amount of de-rating. Looking at consensus, analysts basically see EPS growing at a double-digit clip for years to come. Given the firm's growth drivers (RevPAR growth, system growth, operating leverage and buybacks), this seems reasonable. In addition, Marriott does pay a modest quarterly dividend of $0.67 per share, equal to a yield of 1%. That pushes 'business-level' returns comfortably above a 10% annualized rate, affording investors a margin of safety in the event of modest long-term P/E contraction. On that basis, I am shifting my rating to 'Buy'.

data.title: Touchstone Active Bond Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Recap

Market uncertainty carried over from the first quarter into the second, driven by geopolitical and trade policy developments. The most notable catalyst was the escalation of reciprocal tariffs in early April referred to as “Liberation Day.” A brief resolution followed, easing near-term fears but failing to bring lasting clarity to consumers and businesses navigating capital spending and hiring decisions.

Despite volatility, the economic backdrop remained stable. Retail activity showed signs of resilience, though this was somewhat distorted by front-loading behavior ahead of tariff implementation. Labor market conditions remained healthy, and real wage gains supported consumers. However, the rising personal savings rate suggested growing caution. The housing sector continued to struggle under the weight of high mortgage rates and affordability constraints. Financial markets finished the quarter on firmer footing, with equities recovering to record levels and credit spreads tightening after an initial spike. Inflation expectations eased slightly, prompting a steepening of the yield curve even as the 10-year Treasury yield remained relatively stable.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone Active Bond Fund (Class A Shares, Load-Waived) outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, for the quarter ended June 30, 2025.

The Fund outperformed its benchmark during the quarter, with results driven by positioning across sectors, tactical interest rate exposure, and security selection. The most impactful decision was the addition of high yield corporate exposure in April, implemented amid spread widening tied to trade uncertainty. As risk sentiment improved and spreads retraced, this allocation enhanced returns. Maintaining an overweight to investment grade corporates also supported performance.

Within securitized assets, positioning in collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs) added value relative to agency passthroughs. Broader security selection across the securitized space, particularly within higher-quality segments, further contributed to the Fund’s outperformance.

The Fund’s tactical approach to interest rate exposure was another source of positive relative performance. Rate volatility was elevated during the quarter as expectations for future policy actions shifted. The Fund began the quarter long duration, then adjusted exposures in response to evolving inflation and growth forecasts. These changes aligned well with the steepening yield curve and helped capture relative gains. By quarter-end, the Fund returned to a neutral duration posture, reflecting a belief that longer rates are now within a fair value range. On a yield curve basis, positioning remained largely neutral, though tactical adjustments during the period added modest value as the curve steepened in response to shifting rate cut expectations.

The Fund’s sector profile also evolved, with the high yield corporate allocation increasing the Fund’s risk budget to 40%. This addition was offset in part by reduced Treasury exposure. Otherwise, the portfolio remained focused on bottom-up selection opportunities across core sectors.

Outlook and Conclusion

The Fund is maintaining a modest risk overweight in light of current valuations and market dynamics. Although spreads have narrowed and valuations remain tight relative to historical levels, a selective approach still reveals opportunities within credit sectors. The Fund continues to favor Investment Grade Credit, with a bias toward liquid, higher-quality issuers and selective exposure in areas like midstream and banking.

Within Securitized Products, the Fund remains overweight, concentrating on non-agency commercial mortgage-backed securities and residential mortgage-backed securities.

Although many sectors have returned to tight spread levels, specific opportunities persist, especially within high-quality tranches that offer better risk-adjusted returns.

Emerging market debt exposure remains neutral, as spreads have also tightened and headline volatility continues. The allocation continues to emphasize relative value opportunities within the higher-yielding segments of the emerging market debt space.

The high yield corporate allocation remains intact, though spread tightening has tempered the overall attractiveness. The Fund retains flexibility to adjust exposure as conditions evolve, while maintaining a focus on quality and liquidity.

In terms of rate exposure, the Fund is positioned with neutral duration and curve positioning relative to the benchmark. While volatility is expected to remain elevated, this environment provides opportunities for continued tactical adjustments as new data and policy developments unfold. While economic forecasts for 2025 and 2026 have been revised lower, the recent passage of a federal reconciliation package is expected to provide modest support for near-term activity through tax relief and accelerated capital expenditure incentives. The Fund is positioned to navigate an environment defined by slower growth, elevated volatility, and policy uncertainty. The current credit posture aims to benefit from tighter spreads relative to the benchmark, while allowing room to add risk exposure opportunistically should the economic backdrop deteriorate. The Fund’s emphasis on security selection and tactical duration management remains central to the strategy, offering flexibility to respond to market dislocations while pursuing consistent long-term performance.

Fort Washington is a member of Western & Southern Financial Group

A Word About Risk

The Fund invests in fixed-income securities which can experience reduced liquidity during certain market events, lose their value as interest rates rise and are subject to credit risk which is the risk of deterioration in the financial condition of an issuer and/or general economic conditions that can cause the issuer to not make timely payments of principal and interest also causing the securities to decline in value and an investor can lose principal. When interest rates rise, the price of debt securities generally falls. Longer term securities are generally more volatile. The Fund invests in investment grade debt securities which may be downgraded by a Nationally Recognized Statistical Rating Organization (NRSRO) to below investment grade status. The Fund invests in mortgage-backed securities and asset-backed securities which are subject to the risks of prepayment, defaults, changing interest rates and at times, the financial condition of the issuer. The Fund invests in non-investment grade debt securities which are considered speculative with respect to the issuers’ ability to make timely payments of interest and principal, may lack liquidity and has had more frequent and larger price changes than other debt securities. The Fund invests in U.S. government agency securities which are neither issued nor guaranteed by the U.S. Treasury and are not guaranteed against price movements due to changing interest rates. The Adviser engages a sub-adviser to make investment decisions for the Fund’s portfolio; it may be unable to identify and retain a sub-adviser who achieves superior investment returns relative to other similar sub-advisers. The Fund invests in derivatives and securities such as forward foreign currency exchange contracts, futures contracts, options and swap agreements. Derivatives can be highly volatile, illiquid and difficult to value, subject to counterparty and leverage risks and there is risk that changes in the value of a derivative held by the Fund will not correlate with the Fund’s other investments. Gains or losses from speculative positions in a derivative may be much greater than the original cost and potential losses may be substantial. The Fund invests in foreign securities which carry the associated risks of economic and political instability, market liquidity, currency volatility and accounting standards that differ from those of U.S. markets and may offer less protection to investors. The Fund invests in emerging markets securities which are more likely to experience turmoil or rapid changes in market or economic conditions than developed countries. The Fund invests in sovereign debt securities which are issued by foreign governments whose respective economies could have an important effect on their ability or willingness to service their debt which could affect the value of the securities. The Fund invests in mortgage dollar rolls which involve increased risk and volatility, as the securities the Fund is required to repurchase may be worth less than the securities that the Fund originally held. The Fund may experience higher portfolio turnover which may lead to increased fund expenses, lower investment returns and higher short-term capital gains taxable to shareholders. The Fund invests in Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs) that have risks that largely depend on the type of underlying collateral and risks may include illiquidity, limited active market, the possibility that distributions from collateral securities will be insufficient to make interest or other payments, the potential for a decline in the quality of the collateral, and can bear the risk of default by the loans. Events in the U.S. and global financial markets, including actions taken to stimulate or stabilize economic growth may at times result in unusually high market volatility, which could negatively impact Fund performance and cause it to experience illiquidity, shareholder redemptions, or other potentially adverse effects. Banks and financial services companies could suffer losses if interest rates rise or economic conditions deteriorate. The Fund’s service providers are susceptible to cyber security risks that could result in losses to a Fund and its shareholders. Cyber security incidents could affect issuers in which a Fund invests, thereby causing the Fund’s investments to lose value. Current and future portfolio holdings are subject to change.
